content.text: Plugins are the primary extension point in Semantic Kernel. They bundle one or more related functions — whether backed by Python code, prompt templates, REST APIs, or external tool servers — into a named unit that the kernel can discover, invoke, and expose to LLMs for automatic selection.

Every function registered through a plugin appears in the kernel's function registry. LLMs with tool-calling support can select and invoke these functions autonomously, while your code can also call them explicitly by name.
Semantic Kernel supports four categories of plugins, each suited to a different integration pattern.
Any Python class can become a plugin. Decorate methods with
to expose them; all other methods are ignored by the kernel.
becomes the namespace. Functions are addressed as
Return type annotations matter: the kernel preserves them in the function metadata. LLMs use this metadata, plus the description, to decide when and how to call your function.
For functions that accept structured inputs, annotate parameters with Pydantic models. Semantic Kernel serialises and deserialises them automatically.

The Pydantic model's field descriptions become part of the function's JSON schema, which the kernel sends to the LLM. This lets the model populate the fields correctly from a natural-language request.
A prompt plugin wraps an LLM prompt template as a callable function. The template is written in Handlebars (default) or Jinja2. Input variables declared in the template become function parameters.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.functions import KernelFunctionFromPrompt
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini"))

summarise_fn = KernelFunctionFromPrompt(
    function_name="summarise",
    plugin_name="text_utils",
    prompt="Summarise the following text in {{$max_words}} words or fewer:\n\n{{$text}}",
    description="Summarise a block of text.",
)
kernel.add_function(plugin_name="text_utils", function=summarise_fn)

result = await kernel.invoke(
    "text_utils", "summarise",
    text="Semantic Kernel is an open-source SDK...",
    max_words=20,
)
print(str(result))
Prompt functions can also be loaded from YAML files, which lets non-developers edit prompts without touching Python code.

# Directory structure:
# prompts/
#   text_utils/
#     summarise/
#       config.yaml    (name, description, template_format, input_variables)
#       skprompt.txt   (the prompt template)

kernel.add_plugin(
    parent_directory="./prompts",
    plugin_name="text_utils",
)
OpenAPI plugins import a service's OpenAPI 3.x specification and expose each endpoint as a kernel function. This lets the LLM call external REST services without you writing any wrapper code.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.openapi_plugin import OpenAPIFunctionExecutionParameters

kernel = Kernel()

await kernel.add_plugin_from_openapi(
    plugin_name="petstore",
    openapi_document_path="https://petstore3.swagger.io/api/v3/openapi.json",
    execution_settings=OpenAPIFunctionExecutionParameters(
        http_client=None,   # supply a custom httpx.AsyncClient if needed
        server_url_override="https://petstore3.swagger.io/api/v3",
    ),
)

# The kernel now has functions like petstore.getPetById, petstore.addPet, etc.
result = await kernel.invoke("petstore", "getPetById", petId=1)
print(str(result))
The kernel HTTP client sends real HTTP requests. Ensure the target service is reachable and that any required authentication headers are injected via a custom
When exposing plugins to an LLM, you often want to limit which functions are visible — for security, cost, or relevance. Use filter options on
is safer than relying on exclusions — it ensures newly added functions do not accidentally become callable.
Beyond auto-selection by LLMs, you can invoke any registered function directly from Python. This is useful for testing, deterministic pipelines, and composing functions in prompts.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.functions import KernelArguments

kernel = Kernel()
# ... register plugins ...

# Invoke by plugin name and function name
result = await kernel.invoke("weather", "get_current_weather", city="Paris")

# Invoke via a KernelFunction reference
fn = kernel.get_function("weather", "get_current_weather")
result = await fn.invoke(kernel, KernelArguments(city="Paris"))

# Embed a function call inside a prompt template
result = await kernel.invoke_prompt(
    "Current weather: {{weather-get_current_weather city='Tokyo'}}",
)
print(str(result))
Prompt templates can embed function calls using
syntax. The kernel evaluates these inline during rendering, composing functions directly in prompts.
Because plugins are plain Python classes, you can unit-test their methods without a kernel.

import pytest

class TestWeatherPlugin:
    def test_get_current_weather(self):
        plugin = WeatherPlugin()
        result = plugin.get_current_weather(city="Berlin")
        assert "Berlin" in result

    def test_get_forecast_defaults(self):
        plugin = WeatherPlugin()
        result = plugin.get_forecast(city="Berlin")
        assert "3-day" in result

    def test_get_forecast_custom_days(self):
        plugin = WeatherPlugin()
        result = plugin.get_forecast(city="Berlin", days=7)
        assert "7-day" in result
Integration tests that involve the kernel should mock the service layer (e.g., patch
) to avoid real API calls in CI.
A single kernel can hold any number of plugins. Functions from different plugins can be chained in prompt templates or invoked in sequence from orchestration code.

kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="weather")
kernel.add_plugin(CalendarPlugin(), plugin_name="calendar")
kernel.add_plugin(EmailPlugin(), plugin_name="email")

# LLM can now use all three plugins when answering complex requests
Plugin instances are regular Python objects. Inject dependencies (database connections, API clients, config) via the constructor rather than creating them inside methods.
When a plugin's interface changes, update the
) rather than modifying existing registered functions. This prevents in-flight requests from breaking during a rolling deployment.

Filters let you hook into the execution pipeline for every function call without modifying the function itself. They are analogous to middleware in web frameworks — they wrap invocations so you can inspect, modify, or block them.

from semantic_kernel.filters import FunctionInvocationContext
from semantic_kernel.filters.functions.function_invocation_filter_base import FunctionInvocationFilterBase

class LoggingFilter(FunctionInvocationFilterBase):
    async def on_function_invocation(
        self, context: FunctionInvocationContext, next
    ):
        print(f"Calling {context.function.plugin_name}.{context.function.name}")
        print(f"  Args: {context.arguments}")
        await next(context)                  # call the actual function
        print(f"  Result: {context.result}")

kernel.add_filter("function_invocation", LoggingFilter())
to continue the chain. Omitting it short-circuits the execution — the function never runs. This is how guardrail filters block unsafe calls.
Multiple filters are stacked in registration order. Each filter calls
to pass control to the next filter in the chain, exactly like ASGI middleware.
Prompt rendering filters fire before the prompt template is sent to the LLM, giving you access to the fully-rendered string. Use them to log prompts, enforce PII redaction, or add dynamic context.

from semantic_kernel.filters import PromptRenderContext
from semantic_kernel.filters.prompts.prompt_render_filter_base import PromptRenderFilterBase

class PIIRedactionFilter(PromptRenderFilterBase):
    async def on_prompt_render(self, context: PromptRenderContext, next):
        await next(context)
        # context.rendered_prompt is now available
        context.rendered_prompt = context.rendered_prompt.replace(
            "SECRET_KEY", "[REDACTED]"
        )

kernel.add_filter("prompt_render", PIIRedactionFilter())
The rendered prompt is a mutable string at this point; the LLM sees whatever value you set on
Understanding the auto-calling loop helps you reason about latency, cost, and correctness when an LLM drives multiple plugin invocations.
The kernel sends the user message plus available function schemas (from all registered plugins) to the LLM.
If the LLM emits a tool-call response, the kernel looks up the function by plugin name and function name.
The kernel invokes the function (running all registered filters), captures the return value.
The kernel appends the function result as a tool-result message and sends the updated conversation back to the LLM.
This continues until the LLM produces a final text response (no more tool calls) or the maximum iteration count is reached.

from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import (
    OpenAIChatCompletion,
    OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings,
)
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))
kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="weather")

settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
    service_id="chat",
    function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    max_auto_invoke_attempts=5,   # cap the tool-call loop
)

history = ChatHistory()
history.add_user_message("What is the weather like in Tokyo and Paris today?")

response = await kernel.invoke_prompt(
    prompt="{{$chat_history}}",
    settings=settings,
    chat_history=history,
)
print(str(response))
prevents infinite loops if a misbehaving model keeps requesting function calls without converging on a final answer.
You can introspect the kernel at runtime to list all registered plugins and their functions — useful for debugging, dynamic prompt construction, and generating documentation.

# List all plugins and their functions
for plugin_name, plugin in kernel.plugins.items():
    print(f"Plugin: {plugin_name}")
    for fn in plugin.functions.values():
        print(f"  {fn.name}: {fn.description}")
        for param in fn.metadata.parameters:
            print(f"    param={param.name}, type={param.type_}, required={param.is_required}")

# Retrieve a specific function object
fn = kernel.get_function("weather", "get_current_weather")
print(fn.metadata.description)   # "Get the current weather for a city."

# Generate the JSON schema (what gets sent to the LLM)
import json
schema = fn.metadata.to_function_call_format()
print(json.dumps(schema, indent=2))
Plugin methods can be synchronous or asynchronous. Async methods are idiomatic for I/O-bound work such as HTTP requests or database queries.

import httpx
from semantic_kernel.functions import kernel_function

class GeoPlugin:
    def __init__(self):
        self._client = httpx.AsyncClient()

    @kernel_function(name="geocode", description="Convert an address to coordinates.")
    async def geocode(self, address: str) -> str:
        resp = await self._client.get(
            "https://nominatim.openstreetmap.org/search",
            params={"q": address, "format": "json", "limit": 1},
            headers={"User-Agent": "sk-example/1.0"},
        )
        data = resp.json()
        if data:
            lat, lon = data[0]["lat"], data[0]["lon"]
            return f"{lat},{lon}"
        return "Not found"
The kernel awaits async functions transparently — you do not need to write any special wrapper. Synchronous and asynchronous methods can coexist in the same plugin class.
When a plugin function raises an exception, the kernel wraps it and surfaces it in one of two ways depending on the invocation path.

from semantic_kernel.exceptions import KernelFunctionAlreadyExistsException, KernelInvokeException

# Programmatic invocation: raises KernelInvokeException
try:
    result = await kernel.invoke("weather", "get_current_weather", city="Unknown")
except KernelInvokeException as e:
    print(f"Function failed: {e}")
    print(f"Root cause: {e.__cause__}")

# Auto-invocation by LLM: the kernel appends the error as a tool result
# and sends it back to the LLM, which typically rephrases or recovers.

# Prevent duplicate registration
try:
    kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="weather")   # second time
except KernelFunctionAlreadyExistsException:
    pass  # safe to ignore if intentional
When the LLM is in auto-invocation mode, a function exception is not fatal — the kernel returns the error message as a tool result, and the LLM typically adapts. For programmatic calls, handle
Plugins bundle related kernel functions into a named namespace, making them discoverable by both code and LLMs.
on a Python method registers it;
Pydantic model parameters generate rich JSON schemas, improving LLM argument accuracy.
and YAML-based prompt files create LLM-backed functions without additional Python code.
OpenAPI plugins import REST endpoints from an OpenAPI spec; MCP plugins connect to Model Context Protocol servers.
filters control which functions the LLM can see and call automatically.
Plugins are plain Python — test methods directly, inject dependencies via constructors, and version by renaming the plugin namespace.
